Why is no one blaming the captain for that boneheaded decision? author: @GedionMerso
comment_text:
There is a persistent and unique media lens applied to Arsenal that creates an external pressure other clubs don't face to the same degree.
1st. The "Cojones" Myth; Since Troy Deeney first used the term in 2017 to question Arsenal's "mental minerals," it has become the default setting for pundits.Even when the team is performing at an elite level, this narrative persists.
The other one is, Praise with a Poison Pill: In January 2026, Arsenal won a tense 3-2 battle against Chelsea in a cup semi-final. Instead of praising their grit, pundits like Deeney used it to predict a future collapse, suggesting a potential loss later in the season would "break their spirit" . This is a unique form of criticism—being punished for a win based on a hypothetical future loss.
Moreover;The "Spirit-Break" Theory: After a rare home loss to Manchester United in early 2026, the media focus wasn't just on the defeat (which was only their second in 30 games). The narrative immediately pivoted to "nervous energy" at the Emirates and "title nerves," framing the loss as evidence of an inevitable collapse rather than a statistical anomaly .
The Amorim/Arteta is a perfect example of this double standard and how the media and pundits discuss "the process" of building a team.
When Ruben Amorim took over a struggling Manchester United side, his difficult start was widely met with patience and understanding. Sir Jim Ratcliffe even defended him by pointing to Mikel Arteta's "miserable" first two years at Arsenal .
The hypocrisy lies in the fact that Arteta took over with zero prior head-coaching experience and inherited a fractured squad. Amorim arrived with six years of managerial experience and multiple titles. Yet, Arteta's early struggles are used as a benchmark for "patience," while his current success is still met with skepticism and a "wait until they fail" attitude .

Finally this Media is One of the main outlets pushing the narrative.

author: @nicoperez3834
comment_text:
The complaints from **Brighton & Hove Albion F.C. fans about **Arsenal F.C.’s style of play are honestly a bit rich.

Brighton want to talk about aesthetics, about how the game should be played, about entertainment. That’s easy to say when you’ve played ten fewer matches this season and your squad isn’t stretched across four competitions. Arsenal have been fighting on multiple fronts — league, Europe, domestic cups — and that workload catches up with any team eventually.

When the legs get heavy, football becomes about control, efficiency, and results, not choreography.

The reality is simple: Arsenal are playing for titles. Brighton are playing for compliments.

Nobody lifts a trophy in May because the neutral thought your buildup looked pretty. You win things by managing games, by grinding results, by surviving the long season when fatigue hits and margins get thin.

So if the football isn’t always sparkling, that’s fine.

Arsenal aren’t here to entertain Brighton.

They’re here to win. ⚽

author: @isihenyen
comment_text:
Historical precedent suggests that "negative" tactics are frequently praised as "championship character" or "tactical masterclasses" when they lead to major trophies, but when it comes to Arsenal, bitter rival pundits like Scholes always have something negative to say. 

1. Leicester City (2015–16)
The ultimate "underdog" story was built on a resolute defensive shape and counter-attacking efficiency. 
Tactics: Under Claudio Ranieri, Leicester relied on a rock-solid spine and the relentless defensive work of N'Golo Kanté, who led the league in tackles and interceptions.
Praise: Instead of being labeled "boring," the team was celebrated for its "iron will" and "destiny," with their defensive discipline seen as the key to defying 5000-to-1 odds. 

2. Manchester United (2008–09)
Despite having prime Cristiano Ronaldo and Wayne Rooney, Sir Alex Ferguson’s side won the title through an all-time great defensive foundation. 
Tactics: Managed by the belief that "defense wins you titles," this side set a world record for 14 consecutive clean sheets (1,113 minutes) between November and February.
Praise: They were lauded as "ruthless" and "mentally resilient," with Ferguson’s pragmatic approach cited as evidence of an "elite mentality" required to win three consecutive titles. 

3. Inter Milan (2009–10)
Jose Mourinho’s treble-winning season featured one of the most famous defensive displays in history against Pep Guardiola’s Barcelona. 
Tactics: In the Champions League semi-final second leg, Inter played with 10 men for over an hour and finished with only 14% possession, refusing to keep the ball to avoid losing their defensive shape.
Praise: The performance is widely taught in coaching circles as a "tactical masterclass" of "strategy and defiance" that cemented Mourinho's legacy. 

4. Atlético Madrid (Simeone Era)
Diego Simeone has built a decade of success on "negative" tactics, reaching two Champions League finals. 
Tactics: Employing two deep lines of five to "neuter" lethal attacks, Simeone’s teams prioritize "grit" and "passion" over possession.
Praise: His defensive mastery has been hailed for "dismantling" legendary attacks like Barcelona's "MSN" trio, with many pundits viewing their resilience as the highest form of team chemistry. 

5. George Graham’s Arsenal (Late 80s/Early 90s)
Modern criticisms of Arsenal are often compared to the "1-0 to the Arsenal" era, which is now viewed with nostalgic reverence. 
Tactics: George Graham built a side that was intentionally difficult to beat, focusing on a rigid back four and offside trap.
Praise: While often called "boring" at the time, that era is now cited by current pundits as the "solid foundation" that defined the club's greatest periods of domestic success.

comment_text: Yup, 728 passes, almost 70% possession and only 6 shots on target, averaging 121 passes for every shot on goal. City are so into passing and control.  No urgency to scoring a goal.  Always another pass or dribble is possible.  It's important to run up that pass counter and possession percentage because that shows how good a team you are.  They want to be the Harlem Globetrotters of football. author: @DB2024-k3l
comment_text:
As a Barça fan, I’m stuck in this strange mix of satisfaction and disappointment.
Right after that 4–0 defeat, I said Barça should drop this insignificant trophy, focus on La Liga and the Champions League, and let players rest to avoid injuries.
Everyone jumped on me for saying it.
Fast forward, and now both of our wing-backs are injured.
Congratulations, Hansi Flick, and congratulations to the fans including the clueless ones chanting “we are Barça.

If this had been a Champions League night, I’d have felt proud even in defeat because those are the battles worth fighting.
Losing with heart in the CL means something. But here?
We fought, we overplayed, and we paid the price with injuries… all for a trophy that doesn’t matter in the bigger picture.
Pride without purpose just leaves us weaker when the real nights come.

Yes, the fight showed pride, The only positive outcome from all this mess is that Barça now knows: if they give 100%, they can beat anyone.

This could have been avoided.
we could have rested players instead of overplaying them.
Now imagine the rest of the squad the muscle overload that’s coming from pushing them beyond their limits.
Pride doesn’t win trophies, smart management does. And right now, we’ve sacrificed the bigger picture for nothing.

Now we’ve jeopardized the league and the Champions League… for what?
To chase a meaningless trophy we ended up losing anyway.
The irony couldn’t be louder.

author: @Ac3OrNothing
comment_text: Classic Yamal, the king of stat-padding against the little fish, the invisible man in big games. Unless Villarreal’s on the pitch, this little goblin takes a masterclass in vanishing acts with his ONE shot on target and only TWO created chances in the whole game. He always relies on others finishing in big games instead of doing something on his own for once this entire season. But, of course, the casuals pipe up, "B-bu-but he completed multiple useless dribbles, t-th-that is more important than goals and even winning the cup!" It’s a simple fact that most of Yamal’s career goals are scored against minnows in non-crucial games, he never *consistently* steps up when it counts, NOT counting snagging an occasional goal in a blowout where Barca is already winning by at least 3 goals. Let’s be real: his hype is solely because he wears a Barca kit, just like with Fati and Bojan. Beyond overrated moment merchant.

author: @jskal21
comment_text: Torn both ACLs.  One when I was 18, the other when I was 42.  Both playing.  I have no idea how he could continue playing for 20 minutes after. From a strictly medical perspective (yes, I’m a doctor), if he had no significant joint effusion, bone contusions, meniscal injuries, etc, it’s possible; but I’m still extremely impressed he carried on.  Also, nowadays 6 months is considered early for ACL return to sport.  The graft has to undergo ligamentization, something that was largely overlooked or not talked about until relatively recently.  The graft is almost mush months 3-5 until it strengthens.  Plus the ACL in general has terrible blood supply, let alone to a new graft.  This is the reason R9 re-tore his ACL some 2+ decades ago trying to come back too early from his injury.  I was tough on Neymar and Davies for taking so long to come back after their ACL injuries but now I get it.  I would guess, and it seems, modern medical staffs favor longer recovery periods just based on my observations with Neymar and Davies. I think we will probably see 9 month minimums be the new standard for ACL returns.  Again, just my opinion.

author: @alfredthegreat9543
comment_text:
Ball in play in matches involving Arsenal is the 6th highest. It's a nonsense and untrue narrative. As for corners Chelsea have the quickest average time to take - 28 seconds, Arsenal are the slowest....40 seconds. Literally just 12 seconds longer. Yet VAR takes 4 minutes to make a decision very often. VAR is the biggest problem in football atm - you can't celebrate a goal wholeheartedly in case VAR spots an infringement in build up. It's ridiculous and it needs to go for the good of the game and enjoyment for fans.
PS the reason why some Arsenal games are boring is because they continuously face lower blocks by the opposition than anyone else. It's impossible to playattractive football when the opposition sit deep with 10 men behind the ball on the edge of the box. Why not have a go at the sides who do this? It's obviously their fault because in the CL the opposition try to play football so not one game with Arsenal has been boring.

author: @Matty13Cooper
comment_text:
Hurzeler said "there was only one team that tried to play football today."  Too much like his Swiss father.  Not enough of the German culture there.

If you are going to complain about time wasting, then your team had better not do similar tactics.  Yet, Raya tries to put the ball quickly into play and is being held by a BHA player in this game.  Likewise, Arsenal is awarded a free kick in BHA's half of the pitch and a BHA stands right on top of the ball to prevent a free kick instead of immediately moving ten yards away.

You cannot pick and choose which rules you want to follow to exemplify the way to play football.  Either you follow all or admit your pragmatism and not be critical of others pragmatism.

author: @Upper90FC
comment_text: Full credit to Wolves. But bigger picture, I genuinely cannot wait for this era of U-shaped possession to come to a screeching halt. In all seriousness, sides like Liverpool need to stop obsessing over playing possession football to the extreme. After a certain point, you have to let your players express themselves and just allow them to put the ball into dangerous areas of the box over and over again. And stop allowing low block teams to constantly get back into position by playing backwards and sideways on repeat. Which allows opponents to recover and reduces the quality of shots and crosses. Finally, you know how you combat a low block? Let your low block opponent have the ball and purposely allow them further up the pitch. Which creates more space in behind for you to exploit.

author: @iadvantage2
comment_text: Forget about the youth of these ladies for a moment. Forget about the different club accomplishments, then forget about the games against England, and the Netherlands. I am very impressed with the team right now. Is it the players, or is it the coaches. What we have right here is a rapid development in the standard and the quality of the US women’s soccer overall. It is the finding and meshing of talented players, and a quality coaching staff. The type that you do not wish to play against or be coached against. What we have now does transcend women’s soccer nationally in this country. Have the other club players and coaches not in camp watching? You bet they all are. It does rub off, and that takes us all to another level indeed. I am very impressed with the overall level that awaits us all in the future.

author: @TitleNo34
comment_text:
How many times has Pedro got to get bear hugged by an Arsenal Player, in the penalty area, before he is given a penalty
and how many times are Refs and VAR just going to ignore rugby tackles in a penalty area.
Is it not about time it was all stamped out by Officials.
It is getting ridiculous.
The Ref has a word with the Players not to do it and all that happens is they go back to doing it.
Having said that, Chelsea need to be much, much stronger at corners, for and against.
There also so many 2 yard passes going astray, giving up possession, such a waste.
10 minutes to go, needing a goal and they are piddling about with it at the back.
Chelsea are never, ever going to win another one of the major trophies with this Squad, because they are incapable of beating the big guns consistently, if at all.
Its a shame there are no trophies for the Club that gets the most red cards, CFC would win that hands down.
What a stupid challenge by Neto having already been yellow carded.
Chelsea were somewhat unlucky not to get a point out of it.
Down to 10 men, you would never have known it.

author: @darkanddom
comment_text: Any one brining up the ref's call on Muani last week vs. today's call as inconsistent is missing the nuance. Both were correct calls.  Muani's was a proper call as a foul on Gabriel. Kolo was standing still with his two feet on the grass and his hands on Gabriel's back while Gabriel jumped in the air. You could clearly see the slight push off by Kolo and how it changed the direction of Gabriel's jump. Did Gabriel sell it a little yes but it was a proper call. It was also a proper no foul call today because both the Fulham and Spurs players jumped for the ball and the push happened while both of them were in the air. That is not a foul and so the no foul call was also correct.

author: @GedionMerso
comment_text:
There is a persistent and unique media lens applied to Arsenal that creates an external pressure other clubs don't face to the same degree.
1st. The "Cojones" Myth; Since Troy Deeney first used the term in 2017 to question Arsenal's "mental minerals," it has become the default setting for pundits.Even when the team is performing at an elite level, this narrative persists.
The other one is, Praise with a Poison Pill: In January 2026, Arsenal won a tense 3-2 battle against Chelsea in a cup semi-final. Instead of praising their grit, pundits like Deeney used it to predict a future collapse, suggesting a potential loss later in the season would "break their spirit" . This is a unique form of criticism—being punished for a win based on a hypothetical future loss.
Moreover;The "Spirit-Break" Theory: After a rare home loss to Manchester United in early 2026, the media focus wasn't just on the defeat (which was only their second in 30 games). The narrative immediately pivoted to "nervous energy" at the Emirates and "title nerves," framing the loss as evidence of an inevitable collapse rather than a statistical anomaly .
The Amorim/Arteta is a perfect example of this double standard and how the media and pundits discuss "the process" of building a team.
When Ruben Amorim took over a struggling Manchester United side, his difficult start was widely met with patience and understanding. Sir Jim Ratcliffe even defended him by pointing to Mikel Arteta's "miserable" first two years at Arsenal .
The hypocrisy lies in the fact that Arteta took over with zero prior head-coaching experience and inherited a fractured squad. Amorim arrived with six years of managerial experience and multiple titles. Yet, Arteta's early struggles are used as a benchmark for "patience," while his current success is still met with skepticism and a "wait until they fail" attitude

author: @jaysmith2858
comment_text:
Stevie, stop defending Slot. Are you being paid by FSG for doing some work for the club? You defending Slot makes it sound like you are. The team has looked clueless, vulnerable and lacking in fight and spirit since the backend of last season.

This poor season is largely on Slot. If the team is vulnerable at the back, you change the system or personnel to make you tighter at the back. If a player or a number of players aren't playing well, you bench them and give others a chance e.g. Ngumoah instead of Gakpo or Salah. What you don't do is keep having the same issues game after game and look like you are doing nothing to try and rectify them. Maybe Slot should look at himself and the way he has the team playing before talking about the way other teams and the PL as a whole play.

Enough is enough. Slot needs binning asap. If he is still in charge by the time next season kicks off, Liverpool can write off winning any silverware, and maybe even their future participation in the CL.

comment_text:
Barca absolutely dismantled Atleti today, but it’s a crime they couldn't get that final goal to bring some justice to this tie. Looking back, the whole thing was tainted in the first leg by that '8-minute' VAR review where they manually drew offside lines just to find something the automated system couldn't even see. 

The momentum in this tie was completely sabotaged. Between that disallowed goal and the red card Giuliano was forgiven for after his high kick on Balde, the refs basically handed Atleti a shield. If they hadn't spent 10 minutes killing Barca's rhythm in the first game, this comeback would’ve been completed easily. At least today the world saw who the better team actually is on the pitch. We can be proud of the performance, even if the competition itself feels as shameful as ever. Visca el Barça! 🔵🔴

author: @Worlddiscovery73
comment_text:
I said about 10 years ago. When Messi, Iniesta, Busquets and Xavi are gone from Football (obviously Ronalidinho's Barcelona too), there would be absolutely no reason to watch whole football games anymore. Because they were the absolute epitome of high class technical football. 

Pep in 2017-18, 18-19 had a great team, Liverpool under Klopp were energetic, attacking but never left you dreaming like Barcelona did, or the early Galacticos 2001-2004, or Arsenal between 2001-2006, 2007-08, Man United 98-99, 99-2003, 06-09. You can name AC Milan 2003-2007 too, who were amazing. These teams were the last to make you love football. 

Football needs a total revolution of rules and style. Like no half can finish without a goal, no game can finish without an average of 3-4 goals per game. Bring in volley style entertainment, long distance free-kicks as penalties with no wall. You cannot pay footballers millions a year, charge thouands a year for season tickets, and have an average of just over 2 goals a game. Its crazy. The Bundesliga has constant 3-3 games with great atmosphere. Honestly. Fix it.

comment_text: The issue with barça is that they concede cheap goals, goals that take the least amount of effort to score (ball in behind, pass, goal) over and over. Meanwhile they have to find ways to get past very low blocks and 10 defenders to score their goals. That is not sustainable. You either stop conceding cheap goals like that and make the opponent deploy themselves more to try and score so you have space to work with. Or, you too should sit back and exploit the very same tactics. You can’t have it your way and expect to win trophies like that.

author: @Upper90FC
comment_text: Full credit to Wolves. But bigger picture, I genuinely cannot wait for this era of U-shaped possession to come to a screeching halt. In all seriousness, sides like Liverpool need to stop obsessing over playing possession football to the extreme. After a certain point, you have to let your players express themselves and just allow them to put the ball into dangerous areas of the box over and over again. And stop allowing low block teams to constantly get back into position by playing backwards and sideways on repeat. Which allows opponents to recover and reduces the quality of shots and crosses. Finally, you know how you combat a low block? Let your low block opponent have the ball and purposely allow them further up the pitch. Which creates more space in behind for you to exploit.

author: @learningthroughdialogue932
comment_text:
Poch is kind of like a teacher and the players are the students. In 2025 he was getting pressure to win, which makes sense — coaches always get judged on results.

But sometimes the students don’t do the work. They don’t complete the assignments or master the material. And when that happens, the teacher still gets the blame while people forget about the students’ responsibility.

Some players simply didn’t show up or didn’t perform in 2025.

Poch has already said the USMNT express has left the station and it’s not waiting for stragglers. The spring friendlies are the last chance for some of these guys to prove they belong.

At some point it stops being about potential and starts being about who actually shows up and performs.

author: @theredlightdistrictdaily
comment_text:
This is the inevitable result of relying solely on "clutch moments" rather than a cohesive system. When you face an opponent like Getafe, you already know the script: they will break the rhythm with a relentless barrage of fouls. Madrid’s response, however, lacks any real creativity. Instead of a structured build-up, they fall back on individual brilliance. While Vinícius can take on three defenders at once, Getafe simply neutralizes the threat by bringing him—or his target to pass the ball —down before the play can develop.

Xabi attempted to implement a high-possession philosophy, but the squad wasn't ready for the shift. It feels as though ego and pride are standing in the way of tactical evolution. To succeed, they must play as a collective and complement each other’s movements rather than waiting for a solo miracle.

Furthermore, Madrid fans are quick to mock Barcelona’s style or their heavy historic defeats, like the 2-8. But at least that team maintains a clear identity for a full 90 minutes. Even in a loss, their gameplay remains structured and often "good but unlucky." Madrid, by contrast, seems to vanish for entire matches, becoming invisible the moment they aren't scoring.

author: @Mikel_7595
comment_text:
Matches between big teams always used to be cagey affairs. It has been seen time and again since premier league began that teams used to setup to protect their shapes and not risk opening up trying to attack too much. Set pieces were always an alternative route to score a goal.

And coming to strong vs weak teams, when the weak teams play with 10 men behind the ball and don’t want to score, then they have to be opened up not with a chisel but a sledgehammer i.e, set pieces. 

The idiots complaining about set pieces need to understand the history of football and the current landscape as to how it has changed. The game always evolves for teams to find the best ways to win in the conditions they are playing in.

author: @djdahous
comment_text: Homophobic or baby cry? Same sentence can be interpreted in different ways. In some countries and cultures, some sentences have different meanings. I think that responding to provocations can lead players to say bad things, but we need to understand if something is coming from homophobia or racism or just rivalry or self defense. If vini called prestiani dwarf, isn’t this also discrimination? Can’t prestiani defend himself by calling vini a baby cry? Words shouldn’t always be literally interpreted for their worst meaning. It could have been just provocations without discrimination, homophobic or racism intention’s. If it’s possible to prove that there was indeed true discrimination, then one and possibly these two players must be punished. If someone is lying, they should also be punished. From the players reactions, prestiani seems to be more affected by all of this than the player who was apparently the target of the abuse. But still, both deserve the benefit of doubt until it’s proved their guilt, in case any did speak any discriminatory statements with true deep meaning.

author: @RGraph007
comment_text:
Lamine Yamal is just different. 💎 A hat trick at 18 against a top La Liga side is insane, but what’s actually respectable is how he stays on his feet. While other 'stars' in this league are busy diving for soft pens and baiting the ref, Yamal actually drives through the contact to finish his chances. He’s teaching the critics that you don't need a favorable whistle if you actually have the talent.

Combine that with Pedri—who’s easily the best midfielder in the world right now—and Barca is playing pure football again. No theatrics, no ref protection, just straight class. Bravo Barca! 👏🏻💙❤️

author: @HarbingerOfTruth1
comment_text: Since Jan was incapable of answering Craigs question about what happened to Rosenborg I will have to do it. Rosenborgs problems started in 2011 after the departure of a lot of their key veteran players and in 2012 they got a new chairman named Ivar Koteng. They lost their identity and were unable to retain some icons like they had in the past. However they had a four season period from 2015 to 2018 where they won the league with a manager which then went out to test his luck in Europe. Meanwhile during all this time they consistently flopped in Europe unable to get through qualifiers. In 2022 the chairman Koteng resigned after vast amount of protests from the RBK supporters and they were hoping for a better trajectory. In 2024 they got a new manager in Alfred Johansson which they want to be in it for the long haul with, but their philosophy as a club is no longer as pervasive and they can't keep their talents for long enough. One of their brightest talents of recent times Sverre Nypan was sold to Man City and loaned out to Middlesbrough which has gotten him very little match time. At this moment Rosenborg can challenge for European spots at best in the Norwegian league with more and more Norwegian teams having a better club philosophy and structure than Rosenborg.

author: @Delboy0
comment_text: It is looking like Arsenal Man City final. Man City have improved with the great signing and I feel they are really the best team on the harder bracket. Bayern are overrated as not as good as the Bundesliga makes them look. Real Madrid without Mbappe is an ordinary team. Liverpool and Chelsea are too inconsistent and PSG without Dembele are not the same level. Arsenal are certain to make the final, the only team on their side of the draw who can stop them is Newcastle as I feel they are the only team who can match Arsenal physically. I actually think  Newcastle could be the big shock of the round of People do this every year overestimating the quality of Bayern because they dominate a very low quality League. The tough draw to me means Bayern have no chance of winning it. Bayern are giving up lots of chances to teams who are championship level teams in the Bundesliga, what do you think teams with world class players is going to do them? They won’t get past City 16.

author: @rhythm451
comment_text: I do agree with what Craig said, Barca needs to be calculated and reasoned with their rashford contract situation. Yes rashford has done all right and has helped the team recently through his contributions but all these stories of EPL being angry and Man United being angry because rashford's fee is only 30 million is utter BS... rashford in all honesty is around a 25-30 million player at his current state those rumors are going around to pressure the buyer into thinking that they have a jackpot deal and close it out as fast as possible. Barca already has tons of players on high wages we don't need an extra one that's mainly going to be used as a sub. If It was between having a CB in the summer or better yet a good striker like osimen, then I'd choose one of those instead of rashford easily. If he actually agrees to lower his wages significantly and adding him to the roster won't hurt us from signing someone in the summer then yes add him but if it will then no. I don't care what other fans will say..I'm no fan of players, I'm a supporter of the Club!

author: @geoffreykeenan5914
comment_text:
i think craig's got it all wrong. 
1) rashford's attitude dropped at manu after carrying a team full of players that weren't real, coaches that weren't qualified, and fans looking to blame someone for the fact that the 'inventors' of football were no longer contenders. 
2) aston villa loved his play of game but he was too expensive and had injuries.
3) everything, literally everything, about rashford's time with barcelona indicates that he has no attitude issues whatsoever.

to sum it up, rashford never had attitude issues. he was surrounded by a club and fans that couldn't accept that they were no longer god's gift to football.  that is to say, he was surrounded by others with attitude problems.

and hansi flick will take this to the bank.

and, if barcelona repeat or improve on their extraordinary success of last year, craig won't acknowledge that he's been wrong about rashford and barcelona, and neither will england.

Camavinga was right next to vinicius but didnt react AT ALL!!! WHY? if it was such a bad racist comment, why wouldnt he react??? Mbappe who CLAIMS to have heard him say it FIVE TIMES in such a brief moment (HOW only mbappe knows), at the time also didn't react, but at such an insult at the time didnt react either, only Vinicius started making a scene and afterwards it became a scandal. Valverde who was caught on camera punching a guy in the head got no red card. Mbappe insulting multiple times caught on camera got no sanction either. Vinicius provoking the crowd and hurling nasty insults that were indeed caught on camera didnt get a sanction, but prestianni who has yet to be PROVEN guilty was suspended provisionally (It's not even a real thing).

All im saying is that the sum of the facts presented do not add up.

author: @1honshitsuka
comment_text:
フランクフルトは今シーズンの不振の原因が攻守に穴になってスピードが無く縦突破が出来ない堂安だとやっと気づいたのだと思います。
攻守に穴になってスピードが無く縦突破が出来ない堂安がベンチだった事で、右サイドで崩せるカリムエンドを起用出来たので、左右の攻撃が活性化してチャンスを作る事が出来ていたのです。
そして右サイドに攻守に穴になってスピードが無く縦突破が出来ない堂安がいた場合、縦を崩せないだけではなく守備時に後ろに下がりすぎる事で、相手に押し込まれてしまうと言えます。
フライブルクは右サイドで鈴木唯人を起用していますが、攻守に穴になってスピードが無く縦突破が出来ない堂安に対して、現実を見せつけたと言えます。
